macro_segments.title: Introduction to Toein Vechozer Vetoein and Migo: Theoretical Foundations

micro_segments.title: Setting up the concept: relationship between toein vechozer vetoein and migo
Definition of migo: I could have said something else, versus toein: I'm actually saying something else

macro_segments.title: The Case of David and the Ancestral Field: Direct Application of Migo

micro_segments.title: David claims ancestral plot with witnesses; the speaker contests but lacks direct evidence, having only three years of possession
Rava's migo argument: why should the speaker be believed when he could have claimed a better strategy (purchase with chazakah witnesses)
Abaye's refutation: migo does not overcome direct witness testimony
The speaker's revised claim: adopting Abaye's suggested strategy and claiming he actually did purchase the field from David
Potential problem of toein vechozer vetoein: can the speaker revise his initial claim of ancestral ownership to a claim of purchase

macro_segments.title: Toein Vechozer Vetoein: When Parties May or May Not Revise Their Claims

micro_segments.title: Core debate: Ulla permits revision of claims; Nahardei forbids revision when there is strong contradiction
Ulla's concessions: direct contradictions (ancestral vs. not ancestral) cannot be reinterpreted
Ulla's further concession: revision attempted after leaving court and returning with apparent instruction is not accepted
Nahardei's concessions: reasonable reinterpretations (ancestral plot = father purchased from your father) are accepted even by strict views
Tosafos's approach: the word avotai (ancestral) is not inherently contradictory with a claim of purchase, since both can be true
Distinction between claims made outside court versus inside court: revision of outside claims is permitted even under strict views

macro_segments.title: Modern Legal Application: Comparison to Secular Court Practice

micro_segments.title: David's perspective on how revised claims are treated in contemporary litigation and discovery
Gloria's addition: obligation to provide facts in early litigation stages and difficulty retracting earlier statements on papers
Recognition of parallel between toein vechozer vetoein and restrictions on amending pleadings after factual admissions

macro_segments.title: Second Case: Gloria and Dov with Conflicting Ancestral Plot Witnesses

micro_segments.title: Setup: both parties claim ancestral plot; Dov has witnesses to ancestral plot plus three years of chazakah; Gloria has conflicting witnesses
Rav Nachman's position: contradictory chazakah testimony neutralizes that element; ancestral plot testimony stands unrebutted
Rava's objection: contradicted testimony must be thrown out entirely; witnesses' honesty is questioned
Rav Nachman's response: each witness group retains chazakat kashrut; different aspects of testimony can be accepted separately

macro_segments.title: Debate on Contradictory Witnesses: Rav Huna and Rav Chisda

micro_segments.title: Scenario of two pairs of witnesses directly contradicting each other on the same matter
Rav Huna's position: each pair retains integrity; each can testify separately in different cases despite contradiction here
Critical restriction: if one witness from each contradictory pair testifies together, they form a definitely false testimony and are rejected
Rav Chisda's position: once honesty is questioned, witnesses cannot be accepted even in other matters or other cases
Connection to the Gloria-Dov case: Rav Nachman follows Rav Huna; Rava follows Rav Chisda

macro_segments.title: The Field Case Decision and the Problem of Reversing Judgment: Subsequent Contradictory Evidence

micro_segments.title: Initial ruling: field awarded to Dov based on Rav Nachman's position accepting his ancestral witnesses
New evidence emerges: Gloria brings contradictory witnesses that neutralize all claims
Rav Nachman's willingness to reverse and his concern about zilut bei dina (ill repute of court)
Rava's challenge: are we really not concerned about zilut bei dina? Brings case of woman with contradictory death witnesses
Case of contradictory witnesses to husband's death: woman allowed to remain married if she married after initial permissive judgment
Rabbi Menachem bar Rabbi Yosi's distinction: reversal required only if widow remarried after contradictory witnesses arrived and court warned her

macro_segments.title: Resolution: Rav Nachman's Concession and His Unexpected Action

micro_segments.title: Rav Nachman concedes to Rava's parallel case and announces he will not reverse the field judgment
Rav Nachman then goes out and reverses the judgment anyway; explanation: he found new reasoning (aflei ravrevei—support from great sources)
Teaser: the gemara will reveal what sources or reasoning support Rav Nachman's reversal position

topical_tags.term: toein vechozer vetoein (making a claim and revising it)
migo (I could have said something else, so I'm believed)
mah lo leshaker (what incentive do I have to lie / why should I lie)
zilut bei dina (ill repute of the court)
kol dalim gavar (the stronger party prevails)
